resources.string: All experiments will be permanently deleted.
Try changing the filters\\nor resetting them
Run the first experiment — \\nthe results will appear here.
Launch an experiment so it appears here
Coulomb\'s law is a physical law describing the force with which electrically charged bodies attract or repel each other. The greater the charges, the stronger the interaction.
The Doppler effect is a change in the frequency of a wave (sound, light, or a radio signal) due to the motion of the source or the observer.
The free fall of bodies is defined as motion occurring solely under the influence of the force of gravity.
Harmonic oscillations are oscillations in which a certain physical quantity varies over time according to a harmonic (sinusoidal or cosinusoidal) law.

Joule–Lenz law is a physical law that determines the amount of heat generated by a conductor when an electric current flows through it. It establishes a relationship between the thermal effect of the current and the electrical parameters of the circuit.
A mathematical pendulum is a model of a body suspended from a massless string that oscillates under the influence of gravity.

An oscillator consisting of a rigid body undergoing oscillations in a force field about a point other than the body\'s center of mass, or about a fixed axis perpendicular to the direction of the forces and not passing through the body\'s center of mass.
Motion of a body projected at an angle to the horizontal

The motion of a body projected at an angle to the horizontal is a complex motion in which the horizontal velocity component remains constant, while the vertical component changes under the influence of gravity. Projection from an initial height is supported.

Radioactive decay is the spontaneous transformation of unstable atomic nuclei, accompanied by a decrease in the number of radioactive nuclei over time. The process is described by the exponential decay law.

A spring-mass system is a mechanical oscillatory system consisting of a mass attached to an elastic spring with a specific spring constant, with the other end of the spring rigidly fixed. It undergoes oscillations about an equilibrium position under the action of an elastic restoring force.
Field strength of the first charge
Field strength of the second charge

Two stationary point electric charges in a vacuum interact with a force directly proportional to the product of the magnitudes of these charges and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them.
Let\'s find the electric force using Coulomb\'s law.
Let\'s find the field strength of the 1st charge, which is a vector physical quantity that characterizes the force of the field\'s influence on charged particles at a given point in space.
Let\'s determine the electric field strength of the second charge - a vector physical quantity characterizing the force exerted by the field on charged particles at a given point in space.
Let\'s find the potential energy of the interaction between the charges.
Let\'s find the electric field strength of the first charge.
Let\'s find the electric field strength of the second charge.
Sound frequency as the source moves away
The frequency increases as the source approaches (sound waves are compressed) and decreases as it moves away (sound waves are stretched).
Let\'s find the sound frequency as the objects approach each other.
Let\'s find the sound frequency as the source moves away.
Let\'s find the Doppler shift as the objects approach each other - the change in sound wave frequency caused by the motion of the source or the observer.
Let\'s find the Doppler shift associated with receding motion - the change in sound wave frequency caused by the movement of the source or the observer.
Let\'s find the wavelength of the sound wave as the objects approach each other.
Let\'s find the wavelength of the sound wave as the source moves away.
Let\'s find the Doppler shift as the objects approach each other.
Let\'s find the Doppler shift associated with receding motion.
Velocity at a given moment in time
Using the equations of uniformly accelerated motion, where the acceleration is always constant and equal to the acceleration due to gravity, g.
Let\'s find the velocity at the specified moment in time.
Let\'s find the vertical distance traveled by the body.

The equation of harmonic motion describes the oscillating motion of a physical quantity according to a harmonic law; to address harmonic oscillations, we need to define the quantities that characterize them. Any oscillatory motion can be described by the following quantities: period, frequency, amplitude, and oscillation phase.
Let\'s find the angular frequency - a physical quantity equal to the number of complete oscillations performed in 2π seconds.
Let\'s find the initial phase of the oscillations - a physical quantity representing the displacement of a point from its equilibrium position.
Let\'s write down the equation of harmonic oscillations.
Let\'s find the initial phase of the oscillations.
The amount of heat released by a conductor

Heat is generated when an electric current flows through a conductor. The amount of heat is directly proportional to the square of the current, the resistance of the conductor, and the duration of the current flow.
Let\'s find the current using Ohm\'s law.
Let\'s find the voltage using Ohm\'s law.
Let\'s find the resistance using Ohm\'s law.
Let\'s determine the amount of heat using the Joule-Lenz law, given the current and the conductor\'s resistance.
Let\'s find the amount of heat using the Joule-Lenz law, given the current and voltage.
Let\'s find the amount of heat using Joule\'s law, given the voltage and resistance.
Let\'s find the work done by the electric current.
Let\'s find the magnitude of the electric charge.
Let\'s find the amount of heat.
The period of a mathematical pendulum depends only on the length of the string and the acceleration due to gravity.
Knowing the length of the string and the acceleration due to gravity, we can find the period of oscillation.
Knowing the length of the string and the period of oscillation, we will find the acceleration due to gravity.
Knowing the period of oscillation and the acceleration due to gravity, we can find the length of the string.
Knowing the period, we calculate the linear oscillation frequency.
Let us calculate the angular frequency - the number of oscillations in 2π seconds.
Let\'s find the amplitude of oscillation of a mathematical pendulum.
Let\'s find the maximum speed of the mathematical pendulum; it is reached at the moment it passes through the equilibrium position.
Let\'s find the maximum height to which the mathematical pendulum rises; this is reached at the extreme points of the oscillation amplitude, where the velocity is zero.
Let\'s find the period of oscillation.
Let\'s find the acceleration due to gravity.
Let\'s find the length of the thread.
Let\'s find the linear oscillation frequency.
Let\'s find the amplitude of oscillation of a mathematical pendulum.
Let\'s find the maximum speed of a mathematical pendulum.
Let\'s find the maximum height to which the mathematical pendulum rises.
Moment of inertia of a body about the axis of rotation
Distance from the axis of rotation to the center of mass
Distance from the axis of rotation to the center of mass, m
Length of a simple pendulum with the same period
A physical pendulum takes into account the actual characteristics of the body: its shape, dimensions, mass, and the distribution of that mass relative to the axis of rotation.
Let\'s find the period of oscillation of a physical pendulum.
Knowing the period, we calculate the linear frequency of oscillations.
Let us calculate the angular frequency - the number of oscillations in 2π seconds.
Let\'s find the length of a mathematical pendulum with the same period.
Let\'s find the angular acceleration - the acceleration at the moment when the pendulum is deflected by an angle α.
Let\'s find the restoring moment - the moment of the gravitational force tending to return the pendulum to equilibrium.
Let\'s find the potential energy - the energy of the pendulum at the point of deflection by an angle α.
Let\'s find the maximum angular velocity; it is reached at the moment the system passes through the equilibrium position.
Let\'s find the linear oscillation frequency.
Let\'s find the length of a mathematical pendulum with the same period.
Let\'s find the maximum angular velocity.
The motion can be resolved into two independent components: uniform motion in the horizontal direction and uniformly accelerated motion in the vertical direction, with an acceleration of g.
Let\'s resolve the initial velocity into components along the axes.
Let\'s write down the equations of motion.
At the moment of impact, the height is zero.
Application of the law of radioactive decay, which describes the dependence of the intensity of radioactive decay on time and on the number of radioactive atoms in the sample.
Let\'s find the number of remaining nuclei using the law of radioactive decay.
Let\'s find the radioactive decay constant—a quantity that defines the probability of the decay of an unstable atomic nucleus per unit of time.
Let\'s find the mean lifetime - the average time interval during which a nucleus remains radioactive before decaying.
Let\'s find the number of remaining nuclei.
Let\'s find the radioactive decay constant.
The period of oscillation of a spring-mass system depends on the mass of the load and the stiffness of the spring. The oscillations of the load occur under the action of the spring\'s elastic force.
Knowing the mass of the load and the spring stiffness coefficient, we can find the period of oscillation.
Knowing the mass of the load and the oscillation period, we will find the stiffness coefficient.
Knowing the oscillation period and the stiffness coefficient, we can find the mass of the load.
Knowing the period, we calculate the linear oscillation frequency.
Let\'s calculate the angular frequency—the number of oscillations in 2π seconds.
Let\'s find the period of oscillation.
Let\'s find the weight of the load.
